reasoning: Looking at the noun pairs, the shapes with falling tone on long mid/back vowels (like ôo, âa) occur frequently as members of common patterns (e.g., gâaar/gɛɛ́r, bôook/bóok, dèeŋ/dêeŋ). Among (a)-(f), tôoɲ and ɟâak fit those attested shapes, while the others do not clearly match any shown pattern.
1st person lwɔ̀ɔɔj shows lowered vowel (o→ɔ) and one-step longer length; removing the w gives the root with the next-higher vowel quality and one mora shorter: lòoj.
3rd person keeps the root’s vowel quality (o) and adds one mora of length: lòoj → lòoooj.
Given 3rd cɛ̀ɛm (raised from a to ɛ and lengthened), the root is the shorter, lower grade: càm.
1st person lowers and lengthens from the root: càm → càam.
Root pèec; 1st pɛ̀ɛɛc (lowered, longer). 3rd keeps root quality e and lengthens by one mora: pèeec.
Root wìc; 3rd wìic (same quality, longer). 1st lowers i→e and lengthens: wèec.
3rd bòok is one mora longer with same quality, so the root is the shorter form: bòk.
From root o, 1st person lowers to ɔ and lengthens; w appears with back rounded grades: bwɔ̀ɔk.
Plural rím corresponds to the common pattern ɛɛ → i (seen e.g., djɛɛ → di). So singular is rɛ̀ɛm.
Plural wíil matches the raising pattern from mid-front to high-front with greater length; predict singular wɛ̀ɛl.
Singular àɲâaar (low a-grade) pluralizes by raising a→ɛ with shorter length, as in gâaar→gɛɛ́r.
Singular rɛ̀ɛɛc likely raises to i and shortens, paralleling djɛɛ→di; hence ríic.
Plural kàal fits the pattern ɔ → aa (cf. gɔĺ → gàal). So singular is kɔĺ.
From singular kók, plausible plural grades are one step lower (o→ɔ, with added length: kɔ̀ɔk) or one step higher (o→u with added length: kúuk); both are attested alternations for other nouns.
ràaan follows the a→ɛ raising pattern with shorter length in the plural: rɛɛ́n.
Plural léek often comes from a longer e-grade singular (eee with falling tone), as in ɲêeel→ɲéel; hence lêeek.
Singular ról with o can pluralize by lowering to ɔ and lengthening: rɔ̀ɔl.
Singular jìit̪ commonly lowers to e and lengthens for the plural: jèet̪.
